title: WHY COMPANIES SHOULD WELCOME DISORDER
content_html:
Why companies should welcome disorder

A.

Organisation is big business. Whether it is of our lives – all those inboxes and calendars – or how companies are structured, a multi-billion dollar industry helps to meet this need.

We have more strategies for time management, project management and self-organisation than at any other time in human history. We are told that we ought to organize our company, our home life, our week, our day and seven our sleep, all as a means to becoming more productive. Every week, countless seminars and workshops take place around the world to tell a paying public that they ought to structure their lives in order to achieve this.

This rhetoric has also crept into the thinking of business leaders and entrepreneurs, much to the delight of self-proclaimed perfectionists with the need to get everything right. The number of business schools and graduates has massively increased over the past 50 years, essentially teaching people how to organise well.


B.

Ironically, however, the number of businesses that fail has also steadily increased. Work-related stress has increased. A large proportion of workers from all demographics claim to be dissatisfied with the way their work is structured and the way they are managed.

This begs the question: what has gone wrong? Why is it that on paper the drive for organisation seems a sure shot for increasing productivity, but in reality falls well short of what is expected?


C.

This has been a problem for a while now. Frederick Winslow Taylor was one of the forefathers of scientific management. Writing in the first half of the 20th century, he designed a number of principles to improve the efficiency of the work process, which have since become widespread in modern companies. So the approach has been around for a while.


D.

New research suggests that this obsession with efficiency is misguided. The problem is not necessarily the management theories or strategies we use to organise our work; it’s the basic assumptions we hold in approaching how we work. Here it’s the assumption that order is a necessary condition for productivity. This assumption has also fostered the idea that disorder must be detrimental to organizational productivity. The result is that businesses and people spend time and money organising themselves for the sake of organising, rather than actually looking at the end goal and usefulness of such an effort.


E.

What’s more, recent studies show that order actually has diminishing returns. Order does increase productivity to a certain extent, but eventually the usefulness of the process of organisation, and the benefit it yields, reduce until the point where any further increase in order reduces productivity. Some argue that in a business, if the cost of formally structuring something outweighs the benefit of doing it, then that thing ought not to be formally structured. Instead, the resources involved can be better used elsewhere.


F.

In fact, research shows that, when innovating, the best approach is to create an environment devoid of structure and hierarchy and enable everyone involved to engage as one organic group. These environments can lead to new solutions that, under conventionally structured environments (filled with bottlenecks in term of information flow, power structures, rules, and routines) would never be reached.


G.

In recent times companies have slowly started to embrace this disorganisation. Many of them embrace it in terms of perception (embracing the idea of disorder, as opposed to fearing it) and in terms of process (putting mechanisms in place to reduce structure).

For example, Oticon, a large Danish manufacturer of hearing aids, used what is called a ‘spaghetti’ structure in order to reduce the organisation’s rigid hierarchies. This involved scrapping formal job titles and giving staff huge amounts of ownership over their own time and projects. This approach proved to be highly successful initially, with clear improvements in worker productivity in all facets of the business.

In similar fashion, the former chairman of General Electric embraced disorganisation, putting forward the idea of the ‘boundaryless’ organisation. Again, it involves breaking down the barriers between different parts of a company and encouraging virtual collaboration and flexible working. Google and a number of other tech companies have embraced (at least in part) these kinds of flexible structures, facilitated by technology and strong company values which glue people together.


H.

A word of warning to others thinking of jumping on this bandwagon: the evidence so far suggests disorder, much like order, also seems to have diminishing utility, and can also have detrimental effects on performance if overused. Like order, disorder should be embraced only so far as it is useful. But we should not fear it – nor venerate one over the other. This research also shows that we should continually question whether or not our existing assumptions work.

questions.groupHtml:
Questions 27–34

Reading Passage has paragraphs A–H. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph from the list of headings below.

Write the correct number, i–ix, in boxes 27–34 on your answer sheet.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
strategies for time management
organize company / home life / sleep
seminars and workshops
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn A: “We are told that we ought to organize our company, our home life, our week, our day and even our sleep…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn này nói con người ngày càng được kỳ vọng phải sắp xếp mọi thứ trong cuộc sống để tăng năng suất.
→ Đáp án: vi. What people are increasingly expected to do

headings: Complaints about the impact of a certain approach
Fundamental beliefs that are in fact incorrect
Organisations that put a new approach into practice
Companies that have suffered from changing their approach
What people are increasingly expected to do
How to achieve outcomes that are currently impossible
Evidence that a certain approach can have more disadvantages than advantages
_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.questionHtml:
List of Headings

  1. Complaints about the impact of a certain approach
  2. Fundamental beliefs that are in fact incorrect
  3. Early recommendations concerning business activities
  4. Organisations that put a new approach into practice
  5. Companies that have suffered from changing their approach
  6. What people are increasingly expected to do
  7. How to achieve outcomes that are currently impossible
  8. Neither approach guarantees continuous improvement
  9. Evidence that a certain approach can have more disadvantages than advantages

27_______  Paragraph A

28_______  Paragraph B

29_______  Paragraph C

30_______  Paragraph D

31_______  Paragraph E

32_______  Paragraph F

33_______  Paragraph G

34_______  Paragraph H

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword

businesses fail
stress increased
dissatisfied
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn B:
“Work-related stress has increased.”
“workers… claim to be dissatisfied…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn này nói về các tác động tiêu cực của việc quá chú trọng tổ chức/sắp xếp.
→ Đáp án: i. Complaints about the impact of a certain approach

Bước 1: Keyword
Frederick Taylor
principles
improve efficiency
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn C: “he designed a number of principles to improve the efficiency of the work process”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Frederick Taylor đưa ra những nguyên tắc đầu tiên về quản lý công việc và hiệu suất.
→ Đáp án: iiiii. Early recommendations concerning business activities

Bước 1: Keyword
obsession with efficiency
assumptions
order is necessary
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn D:
“this obsession with efficiency is misguided”
“the assumption that order is a necessary condition for productivity”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả cho rằng niềm tin “muốn hiệu quả thì phải có trật tự” là sai lầm.
→ Đáp án: ii. Fundamental beliefs that are in fact incorrect

Bước 1: Keyword
diminishing returns
further increase in order may hinder productivity
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn E:
“any further increase in order may actually hinder productivity”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Quá nhiều sự tổ chức có thể gây hại nhiều hơn lợi.
→ Đáp án: ix. Evidence that a certain approach can have more disadvantages than advantages

Bước 1: Keyword
innovating
devoid of structure
new solutions
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn F: “These environments can lead to new solutions…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Muốn tạo ra giải pháp mới thì cần môi trường ít cấu trúc và ít phân cấp.
→ Đáp án: vii. How to achieve outcomes that are currently impossible

Bước 1: Keyword
Oticon
General Electric
Google
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn G:
“Oticon…”
“General Electric…”
“Google…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn này đưa ví dụ các công ty áp dụng mô hình linh hoạt, ít cấu trúc.
→ Đáp án: iv. Organisations that put a new approach into practice

Bước 1: Keyword
disorder also has diminishing utility
overused
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn H: “disorder, much like order, also seems to have diminishing utility”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Cả “order” lẫn “disorder” đều không phải lúc nào cũng hiệu quả nếu dùng quá mức.
→ Đáp án: viii

questions.groupHtml:
Questions 35–37

Complete the sentences below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
training sessions
not enough
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn A: “as a means to becoming more productive”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Các seminar hướng tới những người cảm thấy mình chưa đủ năng suất.
→ Đáp án: productive

_blockConfig.noteContent:
• Numerous training sessions are aimed at people who feel they are not [35] enough.
 • Being organised appeals to people who regard themselves as [36]
 • Many people feel [37] with aspects of their work.
sentenceItems: • Numerous training sessions are aimed at people who feel they are not [35] enough.
• Being organised appeals to people who regard themselves as [36]
• Many people feel [37] with aspects of their work.

questions.questionHtml:
• Numerous training sessions are aimed at people who feel they are not 35_______ enough.

• Being organised appeals to people who regard themselves as 36_______

• Many people feel 37_______ with aspects of their work.
questions.question_text: Numerous training sessions are aimed at people who feel they are not 35. ___________ enough.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
appeals to
regard themselves as
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn A:
“self-proclaimed perfectionists”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Những người thích tổ chức thường xem mình là người cầu toàn.
→ Đáp án: perfectionists
questions.question_text: Being organised appeals to people who regard themselves as 36. ___________.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
feel
aspects of work
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn B: “workers… claim to be dissatisfied…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Nhiều người không hài lòng với cách công việc được tổ chức/quản lý.
→ Đáp án: dissatisfied
questions.question_text: Many people feel 37. ___________ with aspects of their work.

questions.groupHtml:
Questions 38–40

Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 38–40 on your answer sheet, write
TRUE  if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE  if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN  if there is no information on this
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
aim at order
without considering value
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn D: “for the sake of organising, rather than actually looking at the end goal and usefulness”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Doanh nghiệp và con người tổ chức mọi thứ chỉ để tổ chức, không xem xét tính hữu ích thật sự.
→ Đáp án: TRUE

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.question_text: Both businesses and people aim at order without really considering its value.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
innovation
distinct roles
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn F: “create an environment devoid of structure and hierarchy”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Bài đọc nói đổi mới hiệu quả hơn khi KHÔNG có cấu trúc/phân cấp rõ ràng.
Câu hỏi lại nói phải có vai trò riêng biệt.
→ Trái nghĩa.
→ Đáp án: FALSE
questions.question_text: Innovation is most successful if the people involved have distinct roles.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Google
inspired by General Electric
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn G chỉ nói: “Google… have embraced these kinds of flexible structures”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Bài không nói Google làm vậy vì được General Electric truyền cảm hứng.
→ Đáp án: NOT GIVEN
questions.question_text: Google was inspired to adopt flexibility by the success of General Electric.
